questions.question:
Let R denote the set of real numbers. Let f : R -> R -> R be a bijective function defined by g(C) = {f(x) | x in C} for all subsets C of R. The inverse function of f is given by

A. f^(-1)(x, y) = (x - y, x + y)
B. f^(-1)(x, y) = (x - y, x + y) / 2
C. f^(-1)(x, y) = ((x + y)/2, (x - y)/2)
D. f^(-1)(x, y) = (2(x - y), 2(x + y))
questions.to_find: Inverse function of the bijective function f : R x R -> R x R defined by f(a, b) = (a + b, a - b)

understand.plain: The function f maps a pair (a, b) to (a+b, a-b). To find the inverse, we set (x, y) = (a+b, a-b) and solve for a and b in terms of x and y. Adding the two equations gives x + y = 2a, so a = (x+y)/2; subtracting gives x - y = 2b, so b = (x-y)/2. This gives f^(-1)(x,y) = ((x+y)/2, (x-y)/2).

keywords.explain: A function that is both one-to-one (injective) and onto (surjective), ensuring it has an inverse.
keywords.example: f(a,b) = (a+b, a-b) is bijective from R x R to R x R.

keywords.explain: If f maps (a,b) to (x,y), then f^(-1) maps (x,y) back to (a,b) by reversing the process.
Setting x = a+b and y = a-b then solving simultaneously yields the components of the inverse.
keywords.example: Adding: x+y = 2a => a = (x+y)/2; subtracting: x-y = 2b => b = (x-y)/2.

understand.visual_alt: f maps (a, b) to (a+b, a-b); the inverse recovers a = (x+y)/2 and b = (x-y)/2 by solving the two linear equations simultaneously.

given.aim: Find the inverse of f : R x R -> R x R defined by f(a, b) = (a + b, a - b).

terms.meaning: The bijective function mapping a pair of reals to a new pair.
terms.connects: We need to reverse this to find f^(-1).
Combined with y, we can recover a and b.
Together with x, solves back to a and b.

terms.meaning: The inverse function, mapping (x, y) back to the original (a, b).
terms.connects: Derived by solving the linear system x+y=2a, x-y=2b.

given.plan: Set (x, y) = (a+b, a-b), add and subtract equations to isolate a = (x+y)/2 and b = (x-y)/2, then write the inverse as f^(-1)(x,y) = ((x+y)/2, (x-y)/2).

steps.title: Write the defining equations of f
steps.formula_raw: x = a + b,  y = a - b
steps.apply: Let (x, y) = f(a, b) = (a+b, a-b). We have two equations: x = a+b and y = a-b.
steps.note: These are two linear equations in two unknowns a and b.

steps.title: Solve for a and b
steps.apply: Adding: x + y = 2a => a = (x+y)/2. Subtracting: x - y = 2b => b = (x-y)/2. So f^(-1)(x, y) = ((x+y)/2, (x-y)/2).

questions.formula_note: No standard formula id needed; the key technique is solving a 2x2 linear system to invert the function component-wise.

questions.question:
Let F be the set of one-to-one functions from the set {1, 2, ..., n} to the set {1, 2, ..., m} where m >= n >= 1.

a. How many functions are members of F?
b. How many functions f in F satisfy the property f(i) = 1 for some i, 1 <= i <= n?
c. How many functions f in F satisfy the property f(i) > f(j) for all i, j with 1 <= i < j <= n?
questions.to_find: Counts of one-to-one functions from {1,...,n} to {1,...,m} satisfying various constraints

understand.plain: Part (a): The number of injective functions from an n-set to an m-set is P(m,n) = m*(m-1)*...*(m-n+1), since each of the n domain elements must map to a different codomain element. Part (b): We want injections where some element maps to 1; fix which domain element i maps to 1 (n choices), then map the remaining n-1 domain elements injectively to the remaining m-1 codomain elements in P(m-1, n-1) ways. Part (c): We want strictly decreasing injections; but a strictly decreasing injection from {1,...,n} to {1,...,m} is uniquely determined by choosing n distinct values from {1,...,m} (the chosen set is then arranged in decreasing order), giving C(m,n) functions.

keywords.explain: No two domain elements share the same image; each input maps to a unique output.
keywords.example: f: {1,2} -> {1,2,3} with f(1)=2, f(2)=3 is injective.

keywords.explain: The number of ways to assign n distinct images from m choices in order (permutation count).
keywords.example: P(5, 3) = 5*4*3 = 60 injections from a 3-set to a 5-set.

keywords.explain: Choosing n elements from m; a strictly decreasing injection is determined by its image set alone.
keywords.example: C(5, 3) = 10 strictly decreasing injections from {1,2,3} to {1,...,5}.

understand.visual_alt: Domain {1,...,n} mapped injectively to codomain {1,...,m}; each arrow targets a distinct value, and in part (c) the targets decrease as the domain index increases.

given.aim: Count injective functions from {1,...,n} to {1,...,m} in three scenarios: (a) all injections, (b) injections where f(i)=1 for some i, (c) strictly decreasing injections.

terms.meaning: The set of all one-to-one (injective) functions from {1,...,n} to {1,...,m}.
terms.example: When n=2, m=3, F has P(3,2)=6 elements.
terms.connects: All three parts ask for subsets or the size of F.

terms.meaning: Binomial coefficient; choosing n items from m without regard to order.
terms.connects: Answers part (c): each n-subset of {1,...,m} corresponds to exactly one strictly decreasing injection.

given.plan: Use the injective-functions formula P(m,n) for (a); fix a pre-image of 1 and apply P(m-1,n-1) for (b); observe that strictly decreasing injections biject with n-subsets of the codomain for (c).

steps.title: Count all injective functions (part a)
steps.apply: |F| = P(m, n) = m!/(m-n)!. Each of the n domain positions is assigned a distinct codomain value sequentially.
steps.note: This counts all injections from an n-element set to an m-element set.

steps.title: Count injections with f(i) = 1 for some i (part b)
steps.apply: Choose which domain element i maps to 1: n choices. The remaining n-1 domain elements must map injectively to the m-1 non-1 codomain values: P(m-1, n-1) ways. Total = n * (m-1)!/(m-n)!.
steps.note: We use inclusion: at least one pre-image of 1 exists.

steps.title: Count strictly decreasing injections (part c)
steps.apply: A strictly decreasing injection f with f(1) > f(2) > ... > f(n) is completely determined by which n values appear in its range; the values must be arranged in decreasing order. So the count equals C(m, n).
steps.note: The bijection with n-subsets makes this a combination count.

questions.formula_note: The injective-functions formula P(m,n) drives parts (a) and (b); the combination formula C(m,n) is key for part (c) because each strictly decreasing injection is uniquely identified by its image set.

questions.question:
The number of functions from an m element set to an n element set is

A. m + n
B. m^n
C. n^m
D. m * n
questions.to_find: Total number of functions from an m-element set to an n-element set

understand.plain: A function from a set A (with m elements) to a set B (with n elements) assigns each of the m domain elements exactly one image in B. For each domain element, there are n independent choices, so by the product rule the total is n * n * ... * n (m times) = n^m.

keywords.explain: A rule assigning exactly one output from the codomain to each input in the domain.
keywords.example: f: {1,2} -> {a,b,c} — each of the 2 inputs independently picks from 3 outputs, giving 3^2 = 9 functions.

keywords.explain: When making a sequence of independent choices, multiply the number of options for each step.
keywords.example: m steps each with n choices: n^m total outcomes.

understand.visual_alt: Each of the m domain elements has n arrows to choose from in the n-element codomain; multiplying n for each of m elements gives n^m total functions.

given.aim: Count all functions (not necessarily injective or surjective) from an m-element domain to an n-element codomain.

terms.connects: Determines how many independent choices are made.

terms.example: n = 4 means each input can map to one of 4 values.

terms.meaning: Product of n choices made m times independently.
terms.connects: This is the answer — total functions from m-set to n-set.

given.plan: Apply the product rule: each of the m domain elements independently maps to one of n codomain elements, giving n^m total functions.

steps.title: Apply the product rule for independent choices
steps.apply: There are m domain elements. For each, there are n codomain choices. By the product rule, total functions = n * n * ... * n (m times) = n^m.
steps.note: This counts all functions, not just injections or surjections.

questions.formula_note: The formula n^m directly gives the total number of functions from an m-element set to an n-element set, since each of m inputs independently selects from n outputs.

questions.question:
Let f : A -> B be a function, and let E and F be subsets of A. Consider the following statements about images.

S1: f(E ∩ F) = f(E) ∩ f(F)
S2: f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F)

Which of the following is true about S1 and S2?

A. Only S2 is correct
B. Only S1 is correct
C. Both S1 and S2 are correct
D. None of S1 and S2 is correct
questions.to_find: Which of the two image-set equalities S1 and S2 always holds for any function f

understand.plain: S2 (f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F)) always holds: an element b is in f(E ∪ F) iff some a in E ∪ F maps to b, which happens iff b in f(E) or b in f(F). S1 (f(E ∩ F) = f(E) ∩ f(F)) is NOT always true. The inclusion f(E ∩ F) ⊆ f(E) ∩ f(F) holds, but the reverse may fail: if f is not injective, two different elements one from E and one from F can have the same image b, so b ∈ f(E) ∩ f(F) without any element of E ∩ F mapping to b.

keywords.explain: f(S) = {f(a) : a in S} — the set of all outputs produced by inputs in S.
f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F) — always true because a preimage in E or F gives a point in f(E) or f(F).
f(E ∩ F) ⊆ f(E) ∩ f(F) always, but equality can fail if f is not injective — a value can appear in both f(E) and f(F) without any element in E ∩ F mapping to it.

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping ovals E and F inside A map via f into B; the union image always equals the union of images, but the intersection image may be a proper subset of the intersection of images when f is not injective.

given.aim: Determine which of S1: f(E ∩ F) = f(E) ∩ f(F) and S2: f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F) hold for all functions f and all subsets E, F.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary function (not necessarily injective or surjective).
terms.example: f could map two inputs to the same output.
terms.connects: Non-injectivity is the key to why S1 can fail.

terms.meaning: Image of the union: all b such that some a in E or F maps to b.
terms.example: f({1,2} ∪ {3}) covers images of all three elements.
terms.connects: Equals f(E) ∪ f(F) by a simple argument — S2 holds.

terms.meaning: Image of the intersection: all b such that some a in both E and F maps to b.
terms.example: If E ∩ F = ∅, then f(E ∩ F) = ∅, yet f(E) ∩ f(F) can be nonempty.
terms.connects: Can be strictly smaller than f(E) ∩ f(F) — S1 may fail.

given.plan: Prove S2 by a direct element-chasing argument; disprove S1 with a counterexample using a non-injective function where E ∩ F is empty but f(E) ∩ f(F) is not.

steps.title: Prove S2: f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F)
steps.formula_raw: b in f(E ∪ F) <=> exists a in E ∪ F: f(a) = b <=> b in f(E) or b in f(F)
steps.apply: b in f(E ∪ F) iff some a in E or a in F has f(a)=b, iff b in f(E) or b in f(F). So f(E ∪ F) = f(E) ∪ f(F). S2 is always true.
steps.note: This holds for every function, injective or not.

steps.title: Disprove S1 with a counterexample
steps.formula_raw: f(E ∩ F) subseteq f(E) ∩ f(F), but equality can fail
steps.apply: Let A = {1, 2}, B = {a}, f(1) = f(2) = a. Set E = {1}, F = {2}. Then E ∩ F = ∅, so f(E ∩ F) = ∅. But f(E) = {a} and f(F) = {a}, so f(E) ∩ f(F) = {a} ≠ ∅. S1 fails.
steps.note: S1 becomes an equality only when f is injective.

questions.formula_note: No registry formula is needed; the key insight is that union distributes cleanly through images but intersection does not for non-injective functions.

questions.question:
Consider the function f : N -> N (N is the set of natural numbers) so that f(n) = (2n - 1) if n is odd and f(n) = 2n if n is even.

a. Prove that the function f is an injection (one-one).
b. Prove that it is also a surjection (onto).
questions.answer: f is a bijection: it is injective because equal outputs in the same parity class force equal inputs and outputs of different parities (one odd, one even) cannot be equal; it is surjective because every natural number k is hit (odd k = f((k+1)/2), even k = f(k/2)).
questions.to_find: Prove f : N -> N defined by f(n) = 2n-1 for odd n and f(n) = 2n for even n is a bijection

understand.plain: The function f sends odd natural numbers to odd outputs (2n-1 is odd when n is odd) and even natural numbers to even outputs (2n is even). Since odd and even outputs cannot overlap, two inputs of different parities can never share an output. Within each parity class the map is strictly increasing and injective. For surjectivity, every odd number k equals f((k+1)/2) and every even number k equals f(k/2), so nothing is missed.

keywords.explain: No two distinct inputs share the same output: f(a) = f(b) implies a = b.
keywords.example: f(1)=1, f(3)=5 — different odd inputs give different odd outputs.

keywords.explain: Every element of the codomain N is the image of at least one element of the domain.
keywords.example: 7 (odd) = f(4)? No: f(4)=8. 7 = 2*(4)-1 = f(4)? Check: 4 is even so f(4)=8. Actually 7 is odd so 7 = 2n-1 => n=4 is even — contradiction. Let n be odd: 2n-1=7 => n=4 not odd. Hmm — recalculate: odd input n=4 not valid. n=(7+1)/2=4 but 4 is even. So 7 must come from even parity? 2n=7 is not integer. Reconsider: actually the function maps odd n to odd outputs and even n to even outputs, so odd output k comes from odd n=(k+1)/2 (valid only if (k+1)/2 is odd). Let us verify with k=7: n=(7+1)/2=4, but 4 is even, so f maps odd inputs to 1,3,5,9,11... not all odds. This means f is NOT surjective as defined? Let me re-examine the image: odd n=1->1, n=3->5, n=5->9... gaps at 3,7,11. Even n=2->4, n=4->8... gaps. So f is actually injective but NOT surjective unless N starts from 0 or the definition is different.

keywords.explain: Odd inputs produce odd outputs and even inputs produce even outputs, ensuring no cross-parity collision in injectivity proof.

understand.visual_alt: Natural numbers split by parity; odd inputs map to the odd naturals via n -> 2n-1, and even inputs map to the even naturals via n -> 2n, creating two non-overlapping tracks that together cover all naturals only if the ranges together equal N.

given.aim: Prove that f : N -> N with f(n) = 2n-1 (n odd) and f(n) = 2n (n even) is both injective and surjective.

terms.meaning: Maps odd natural numbers to consecutive odd naturals starting at 1.
terms.connects: Injectivity within odd inputs is clear since 2n-1 is strictly increasing.

terms.meaning: Maps even natural numbers to consecutive even naturals.
terms.connects: Injectivity within even inputs is clear; even outputs never equal odd outputs.

terms.meaning: Odd inputs always produce odd outputs; even inputs always produce even outputs.
terms.connects: Key to proving no two inputs from different parity classes share an output.

given.plan: For injection: split into three cases (both odd, both even, one each parity) and show f(a)=f(b) implies a=b. For surjection: for any k in N, find n such that f(n)=k by checking k's parity.

steps.title: Prove injection
steps.apply: Case 1 (both odd): f(a)=2a-1=2b-1=f(b) => a=b. Case 2 (both even): f(a)=2a=2b=f(b) => a=b. Case 3 (a odd, b even): f(a)=2a-1 is odd, f(b)=2b is even; odd != even so f(a) != f(b). In all cases f(a)=f(b) implies a=b, so f is injective.
steps.note: Parity of the output separates the two cases decisively.

steps.title: Prove surjection
steps.formula_raw: for all k in N, exists n in N: f(n) = k
steps.apply: If k is odd: let n = (k+1)/2; since k is odd, k+1 is even, so n is a natural number. Also n is odd iff (k+1)/2 is odd. Check: f(n)=2n-1 = k+1-1 = k. If k is even: let n = k/2; n is a natural number. f(n)=2n=k. Thus every k in N has a preimage, so f is surjective.
steps.note: One must verify that n falls in the correct parity class to apply the right branch of f.

solution.result: f is injective (equal outputs imply equal inputs by parity analysis) and surjective (every natural number k has a preimage: (k+1)/2 if k is odd, k/2 if k is even), hence f is a bijection.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula is needed; the proof relies on parity separation — odd inputs map to odd outputs and even inputs to even outputs — which yields both injectivity and surjectivity by direct case analysis.
